findings.title: Dead branch in _parse_merge UPDATE handling
findings.description: `col_refs = list(rhs.find_all(exp.Column)) if isinstance(rhs, exp.Expression) else []` then immediately `if not col_refs and isinstance(rhs, exp.Column): col_refs = [rhs]`. Because `exp.Column` is a subclass of `exp.Expression`, the first branch will always execute when `rhs` is an `exp.Column`, and `find_all(exp.Column)` on a Column node returns that node itself. The second branch therefore can never be reached. The same dead pattern appears for the INSERT branch at lines 634-635. A future maintainer who reads the recovery clause will incorrectly assume the `find_all` guard can miss top-level Column nodes — and may write new code around that false belief.
findings.suggestion: Remove the guard-and-retry pattern entirely. `col_refs = list(rhs.find_all(exp.Column))` is sufficient in both UPDATE and INSERT branches. Add a brief comment if the intent was defensive against a specific SQLGlot version behavior.

findings.title: _parse_select_node is doing too many things
findings.description: The function now handles: (1) FROM table collection and alias-map construction, (2) JOIN table collection and alias-map extension, (3) LATERAL VIEW expansion into a lookup dict, (4) a closure `_resolve_table_hint`, (5) JOIN ON predicate → __joinkey__ edges, (6) WHERE → __filter__ edges, (7) SELECT column iteration with six distinct expression-type branches including window, literal, and lateral-expand sub-branches. That is seven distinct responsibilities in ~285 lines. The lateral-view block (lines 298-329) and both the join-key and filter emission blocks are self-contained and can each be extracted without changing any caller. The increasing nesting depth (function-level → for-loop → if-branch → inner loop) makes it hard to follow exactly which edges are emitted and when the `qualified` flag is set.
findings.suggestion: Extract three helpers: `_build_source_alias_map(select_node, cte_map) -> (alias_map, source_tables, edges_from_subqueries)`, `_emit_lateral_view_edges(select_node, alias_map, source_tables, ...) -> lateral_expand dict`, and `_emit_predicate_edges(on_exprs, where_clause, ...) -> list[LineageEdge]`. `_parse_select_node` then becomes an orchestrator of 4–5 clearly-named steps. This does not require changing any external interface.

findings.title: _parsed_files now includes warning-only files — silent semantic shift
findings.description: `entry['_parsed_files'] = set(file_stats.keys())` where `file_stats` is populated first from graph edges (files with lineage) and then from warnings (files with parse errors, no edges). This means `_parsed_files` now tracks files referenced by warnings even if they produced zero edges. The delete and refresh handlers use `_parsed_files` to decide which graph edges belong to this source and should be removed. For files that only appear via warnings (zero edges), the edge-removal loop is a no-op, so correctness is preserved. However the name `_parsed_files` now means 'files seen during this parse cycle (with or without edges)' rather than 'files that contributed edges', and that distinction is nowhere documented. A reader trying to understand the delete logic has to trace both population sites to confirm safety.
findings.suggestion: Rename to `_source_files` or document the broader meaning in a one-line comment at the assignment site: `# includes files with only warnings and no edges`. Alternatively keep a separate `_error_only_files` set distinct from the graph-contributing set if the distinction ever matters for correctness downstream.

findings.title: Untyped dict[str, dict] for _file_stats with implicit schema
findings.description: The `file_stats` structure has an implicit three-key schema `{edge_count, approximate_count, warning_count}` that is written in `refresh_source` (lines 233-251) and read in `list_source_files` (lines 154-165). Both sites must independently know the exact key names — there is no type that enforces this. A typo or a future addition (e.g. a `parse_time_ms` field) requires updates in two places with no compiler aid. The entry is stored on the opaque `state.source_registry` dict, making it even harder for a new contributor to discover the expected shape.
findings.suggestion: Define a `TypedDict` (e.g. `class FileStats(TypedDict): edge_count: int; approximate_count: int; warning_count: int`) in `lineage/models.py` or a new `api/types.py`. Change the annotation at the write site to `dict[str, FileStats]` and at the read site to `entry.get('_file_stats', {})` typed accordingly. This is a two-file change that eliminates the implicit contract.

findings.title: Module-level mutable counter is non-obvious and non-resettable
findings.description: `_subquery_counter: list[int] = [0]` is a module-level singleton used to generate subquery aliases. It persists across calls to `parse_sql`, meaning alias names (`__sub_1__`, `__sub_2__`) increment monotonically across multiple files parsed in the same process lifetime. This is harmless today because aliases are only used as local temporaries within a single `_parse_single_statement` call. But the pattern is misleading: the counter looks like shared state that 'accumulates', and future code that checks alias names for equality across parse calls will behave unexpectedly. There is no way to reset it between test cases without reaching into the module internals.
findings.suggestion: Pass a counter or a factory function as a parameter to `_process_subquery`, or generate aliases from a local `itertools.count()` created fresh in `_parse_single_statement`. This eliminates cross-call state while keeping the same interface.

findings.title: Case-folded lookup uses original temp_views set — inconsistent normalization
findings.description: Inside `_lookup`, line 739: `if src_tbl not in temp_views:` — here `temp_views` is the *original* (non-lowercased) set passed to `_resolve_temp_views`, not `temp_views_lower` which was constructed at line 717. The guard is checking a non-lowercased set against a value that was already lowercased via `src.lower()` on line 735. If the original temp view name had any uppercase letters, this guard will always pass (i.e. never short-circuit), and the function falls through to the wildcard-lookup that may return `None`. The resolved wildcard-fallback at line 754 uses `src_tbl` (lowercased) against `tv_sources` (also lowercased keys), so the actual data path is correct, but the early-exit guard silently misfires for mixed-case view names.
findings.suggestion: Change line 739 to `if src_tbl not in temp_views_lower:`. `temp_views_lower` is in scope as a closure variable from the outer function.

findings.title: Duplicated column-resolution pattern across join-key and filter blocks
findings.description: The JOIN ON predicate loop (lines 345-373) and the WHERE clause loop (lines 378-407) are structurally identical: iterate `col_ref in expr.find_all(exp.Column)`, resolve `table_hint` via `_resolve_table_hint`, compute `qualified`, build a `source_col` string, dedup via a `seen` set, and append a `LineageEdge`. The only differences are the target pseudo-column suffix (`__joinkey__` vs `__filter__`) and the `transform_type` literal. Maintaining them separately means any change to resolution logic must be applied in both places.
findings.suggestion: Extract a helper `_emit_predicate_col_edges(exprs, target_suffix, transform_type, ...)` that handles one or more predicate expressions. Call it twice with the appropriate suffix. The dedup set can be scoped inside the helper.

findings.title: Graph edge removal block duplicated verbatim in delete_source and refresh_source
findings.description: The pattern `edges_to_remove = [(u,v) for u,v,d in graph.edges(data=True) if d.get('data') and d['data'].source_file in old_files]` followed by `graph.remove_edges_from(...)`, then orphan-node removal, appears identically four times: once for `state.lineage_graph` and once for `state.raw_graph`, in both `delete_source` and `refresh_source`. Any change to the pruning logic (e.g. filtering by source_id instead of file path) must be replicated four times.
findings.suggestion: Extract `_remove_source_files_from_graph(graph: nx.DiGraph, files: set[str]) -> None` and call it in both handlers for both graphs.

findings.title: Node-style computation for column and table views duplicated inline
findings.description: The collapsed and expanded branches of the `rfNodes` useMemo (lines 159-223) each independently compute `isTarget`, `isSource`, `isSink`, and then the same three-branch bg/border/color assignment. The logic is slightly different (column view checks pseudo-node suffix; table view doesn't), but the bg/border/color pattern is copy-pasted. If the color palette changes, both branches need updating.
findings.suggestion: Extract `nodeStyle(role: 'target' | 'source' | 'sink' | 'pseudo' | 'default')` returning the style object. Both branches call it with the computed role. The role determination logic remains separate (it legitimately differs).

findings.title: Filter and join-key edge counts computed by scanning full edges array twice
findings.description: `const filterEdgeCount = edges.filter(...).length` and `const joinKeyEdgeCount = edges.filter(...).length` perform two separate O(n) scans outside of any useMemo, so they recompute on every render. The `edges` prop is typically stable between renders (parent memoizes), but the inline scans are not memoized themselves and will re-run on unrelated state changes (`collapsed`, `showFilters`, `showJoinKeys`).
findings.suggestion: Wrap both in a single `useMemo(() => { let f=0, j=0; for (const e of edges) { ... } return {f, j}; }, [edges])` to compute both counts in one pass and memoize the result.

residual_risks.area: Temp-view chain resolution correctness under mixed case
residual_risks.description: Finding M-06 identifies that the `_lookup` guard uses the non-lowercased `temp_views` set. If this is not fixed, chains involving mixed-case view names silently fall through the guard to the wildcard path and may return `None` when the exact-match path would have succeeded. The wildcard fallback may still produce a correct result in many cases, but the failure mode is silent and difficult to detect through test coverage alone.

residual_risks.area: Module-level subquery counter state across test cases
residual_risks.description: The `_subquery_counter` module singleton (M-05) means test isolation requires either importing the counter and resetting it manually, or re-importing the module between tests. Test suites that check alias names in emitted edges will produce different values depending on test execution order.

Without a TypedDict (M-04), any future contributor adding a new stat key to refresh_source and forgetting to handle the default in list_source_files will cause a KeyError at the /sources/{id}/files endpoint, surfaced only at runtime.

testing_gaps.description: No apparent test for the dead recovery branch in _parse_merge (M-01). If the branch were ever needed (e.g. due to a SQLGlot change where find_all on a leaf node returns empty), the silent data loss would be undetected.
No test covering a mixed-case CREATE TEMP VIEW name followed by a consumer SELECT that references the view in different case — the path that exercises the M-06 guard misfire.
The PIVOT emission logic in _process_subquery (lines 202-223) is complex but there is no visible coverage of the PIVOT-with-multi-output-column case in the test files referenced in CLAUDE.md.
